titlestmt.titleproper: Guide to the Monson Family Invoices
titlestmt.author: by Sandra Markham

descriptivenote.p: Finding aid description and metadata in Archives at Yale are licensed under a CC0 1.0 Universal (CC0 1.0) Public Domain Dedication. This means that anyone may copy, modify, and distribute this descriptive metadata without restriction or permission. The CC0 license is explained in full on the Creative Commons website: CC0 1.0 Universal (CC0 1.0) Public Domain Dedication. More information is available on our Help page.

maintenanceevent.eventdescription: This finding aid was produced using ArchivesSpace on Wednesday March 2, 2022 at 02:02

did.abstract: The collection holds more than 170 invoices and receipts issued to members of the wealthy and aristocratic Monson family of London and Lincolnshire, between the years 1730 and 1834.

accessrestrict.head: Conditions Governing Access
accessrestrict.p: This material is available for research.

bioghist.head: Monson Family
bioghist.p: The Monson family were settled in Lincolnshire from the fourteenth century, and by the eighteenth century they also had a home on Albemarle Street in London. The papers in the collection relate to five generations of the family: John Monson (c. 1693–1748), the first Baron Monson, and his wife Margaret Watson (1696–1752); their eldest son John (1727–1774) and his wife Theodosia Maddison (1725–1821); their son John Monson (1753–1806) and his wife Elizabeth Capel (1755–1834); their son John George Monson (1785–1809) and his wife Sarah Savile (1786–1852); and their son Frederick John Monson (1809–1841). As the invoices and receipts are made out only to Lord Monson or Lady Monson, their life dates may assist in identifying the shopper in question.

prefercite.head: Preferred Citation
prefercite.p: Monson Family Invoices. The Lewis Walpole Library, Yale University.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The collection holds more than 170 invoices and receipts issued to members of the wealthy and aristocratic Monson family of London and Lincolnshire, between the years 1730 and 1834. The documents are engraved, printed, and manuscript invoices, many with illustrated billheads, that are often accompanied by manuscript receipts for payments received by the vendors. They are organized by four categories: Food and beverages; Clothing and accessories; Household expenses; and Other Expenditures. The latter group includes music lessons, transportation (primarily by sedan chair), charitable contributions made to St. George's Hospital in London, and other goods and services. Most of the vendors and purveyors were located in London and are noted at the folder level in the collection finding aid; several of the merchants were women such as Hannah Glasse (1708-1770), in particular those selling clothing, headwear, and textiles. A folder of household receipts document the expenses of a ball held by Lord and Lady Monson at their London home on June 22, 1807.

userestrict.head: Conditions Governing Use
userestrict.p: The Monson Family Invoices is the physical property of the Lewis Walpole Library, Yale University. Literary rights, including copyright, belong to the authors or their legal heirs and assigns. For further information, consult the W. S. Lewis Librarian/Executive Director.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Series I is organized into five subseries: Food and beverages; Clothing and accessories; Household expenses; Other Expenditures; and Receipts.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: A dozen invoices and receipts record the family's purchases of wine, cider, various waters (Bristol, Pyrmont, Lavender) and seltzer from the purveyors Rene Baudowin, Patt Broders, Thomas Davis, Francis Stowells, Bartholomew Valle and Brother, and John Lambe. Also present is one engraved trade card for asses milk sold by William Guest in Knightsbridge.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Three invoices, two for corks from Wheldon & Cuthurst and Benjamin Ingram, and one for bottles from The Royal Northumberland Bottle Warehouse; all vendors were in London.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Nine invoices for cheese, fruits, and other articles of food purchased from purveyors including Bartholomew Valle and Brother, Dance & Co. Orange Merchants, and John Hyde & Co. Most of the invoices are printed checklists of shop offerings, with items received written on the verso.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Nine engraved invoices dating from 1775 to 1784 from Stephen Wright and S. Wright & N. Dupree, Poulterers to their Royal Highnesses the Dukes of Gloucester and Cumberland; their billheads featuring a Royal Warrant were engraved by Marly. Also present is a single manuscript receipt dated 1807 from Edward Barr, who had sent poultry to the Monsons in London.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Thirteen invoices, most engraved illustrated billheads, from shops covering a wide assortment of clothing and accessory items for adults and children. Shops include Elizabeth Hebert & Co.; Gubbins and Guest (baby clothing); Robert Blunt; Isaac Wright; and the stay makers Mrs Schram, Bricart & Girardet and James Gayleard. Of note are three 1754 invoices from Hannah Glasse (1708-1770), Habit Maker to the Princess of Wales. Also of note is one invoice from the Masquerade Warehouse of J. Thresher from which Lady Monson purchased a domino and two silk masks in 1808. Most of the invoices in the folder are addressed to the ladies Monson.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Eight receipts and invoices for hats, caps, and bonnets for family members. One engraved illustrated tradecard is present for Peter (Pierre) Le Quainter, in both English and French and featuring his golden ball and star trade sign, and for Elizabeth Dyer, whose offerings range from Leghorn and Bermuda hats to painted floorcloths.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Two engraved invoices and one manuscript receipt from James Wakeman, umbrella and parasol manufacturer.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Two engraved invoices from Henry Otley and Jonathan Andrews in London.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The largest group of documents in the collection are the twenty-five invoices and receipts from a ball that Lord and Lady Monson held in their London home on the evening of June 22, 1807. Two manuscript account slips at the front of the folder list categories of disbursements for musicians, porters, waiters, flowers, cooks, and ornaments. The single engraved invoice is from the Calton Cut & Plain Glass Manufactory which offered for rent chairs, tables, and "every other necessary article for Routs and Balls." From that firm the Monsons procured, among other items, 96 feet of tables, 206 feet of benches, and 4 dozen chairs in addition to tableware including 14 dozen decanters, 20 dozen wines and 20 dozen goblets. Receipts are also present for food including ham, poultry, fruits, and spices, for confectionary and baking services, and for "Chalking the Floors of Two Rooms for Ball." Nearly all of the papers are noted on their versos "for the Ball" or "for the Ball night."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: An invoice and receipt from carpet manufacturer Thomas Norris, and a receipt from William Barton.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: One engraved invoice from London (Edward Wood & Co.) and one manuscript receipt from Lincolnshire.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: One engraved and illustrated certificate of insurance from the Sun Fire-Office in London to John George Monson (1785–1809), the 4th Baron Monson, covering his home in Burton, Lincolnshire, and the household goods, wearing apparel, prints, books, and plate therein.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Two engraved invoices, one for wallpaper and borders from Bromwich, Isherwood & Bradley, and one for a chair and upholstery from Charles Smith & Co.; both firms were in London.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Eleven engraved invoices and manuscript receipts for objects including ice moulds, cutlery, lamps, and flower baskets purchased, as well as for repairs and services hired. Vendors include Thomas Chamberlain, Argand & Elgar, William Boutel, Thomas Swain, Thornhill & Morley, and G. Wood Iron Founder, Smith, & Furnishing Ironmonger. The illustrated bill heads feature images of tools of their trade, and their products such as flower baskets, fencing, a fire grate, a door lock, and a bell.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: One manuscript receipt paid by Lord Monson, guardian of Lewis Monson (later Lewis Watson, Baron Sondes) for the services of Daniel Hooks and Partners, for "catching of wratts about Lees Court"; Lewis Monson (1728-1795) was the second son of John Monson and Lady Margaret Watson.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Six printed receipts covering taxes for water, windows, land, and sewage in London.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Nineteen engraved invoices and manuscript receipts for yard goods, trim, lace, buttons, upholstery fabric, and tablecloth, from London mercers and silk weavers. The firms include James Brown, Paul Desbrosses & Son, Daniel Vial, Tresilian's & Ashburner, Vale's, Powell & Hall, James Savage, Dyde & Scribe, Pritchard & Dudding, Mary Oliver, E. Bunting, Shoolbred, Cook & Company, and Van Commer, Chavany Paul & Niccolls. Some of the receipts are for cleaning, starching, and reglazing items including blankets and old quilts. Also present is one printed invoice from E.S. & R. G. Francis in Lincoln.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: One invoice for stretched canvases, colors, and frames from Henry Brookes, London.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: A single engraved illustrated invoice from John Higginbotham, fishing tackle manufacturer on the Strand.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Nine receipts and invoices for music books and scores, instrument strings, music and dancing lessons, and for the use or hire of instruments. The receipts include one for four unrelated books acquired from the publisher J. Debrett.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Seven engraved invoices for donations "for the relief of the Lame, Sick & needy" in the hospital from both Lord and Landy Monson.

did.unittitle: Subscription to the Racing Calendar for 1807

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Eight invoices and receipts, primarily manuscripts, for various transportation expenses. Of note are two accounts of Lady Monson's travel by sedan chair in May and June 1805

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Fifteen manuscript receipts, many of which were formerly attached to invoices likely in the folders above.
